notes.note: Philotimus, a freedman of Terentia who handled Cicero's finances; his disappearance and mismanagement are a running anxiety of book 11. Cicero never names him here, but he is the agent who left the household accounts in disorder.

The cistophorus was a silver coin of the province of Asia, named for the mystic basket (cista) of Dionysus on its reverse. Cicero held a large balance in this Asian currency, recoverable through Atticus's banking connections.
About 2,200,000 sesterces, a very large sum (a comfortable equestrian census was 400,000). Cicero hopes Atticus can transfer it by bill of exchange to shore up his credit while he is stranded.

notes.anchor: if those with whom I am stand safe
notes.note: A guarded reference to the Pompeian side, whose camp Cicero had joined; he writes obliquely because the letter could be intercepted by Caesar's men.
